---
title: "Where are feeder-cattle prices this week vs. recent history?"
subtitle: "Weekly time series · Clovis, NM auction"
toc: true
toc-depth: 2
---

::: {.callout-tip appearance="simple"}
**Caveat before scrolling.** This page shows actual reported weekly prices, not predictions. The chart answers *"where are we now compared with the last few months / years?"* — the trajectory itself, not a recommendation about what comes next. See [Methodology](methodology/index.qmd) for the data window, the deflation choice, and what the lines aggregate.
:::

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
**Aggregation note for multi-lot weeks.** This page currently simple-averages multiple lots within the same (week, class, weight bin) cell. The other chart-shelf pages (price–weight, seasonality, basis, sell-now compare, one-pager) use head-weighted means. Where lot sizes vary substantially within a week, the simple-average value can differ by ~$1-3/cwt from the head-weighted version. A head-weighted refactor is queued; the methodology page documents the choice.
:::

## About this page

The third view in the chart shelf. The [Price–weight explorer](price-weight.qmd) answers *"what does each weight class fetch on average?"* and the [Weekly seasonality](seasonality.qmd) page answers *"when in the year do these prices peak?"* This page answers a different question: *"how have prices moved week-by-week since October 2017?"* The chart shows actual auction-week observations on a calendar timeline, with one line per weight class so a producer can see at a glance whether this week sits high, low, or middle relative to recent history. Two button rows above the chart let you pick the **mode** (top: Nominal $ / Real December-2025 $) and the **year window** (bottom: All / Last 5 years / Last 3 years / latest calendar year). Each row maintains its own selection independently — at any moment, the chart shows the combination of both highlighted choices.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| label: data-load

# Load via the shared Clovis loader — same as price-weight.qmd and
# seasonality.qmd. Both eras (Era B TXT 2017-2019, Era A MARS 2019+)
# read together as one combined weekly series.

from pathlib import Path
import sys
import pandas as pd

PROCESSED = Path("..") / "data" / "processed"
CPI_PATH = PROCESSED / "cpi_latest.parquet"

sys.path.insert(0, str(Path("..").resolve()))
from pipelines.clovis.load import load_clovis_combined  # noqa: E402

data_is_placeholder = False
data_source_label = None

_loaded = load_clovis_combined(PROCESSED)
if _loaded is not None:
    df = _loaded.df
    data_source_label = _loaded.data_source_label
else:
    # Synthetic stand-in. Clearly labelled.
    import numpy as np
    rng = np.random.default_rng(seed=2026)
    rows = []
    bins = ["300–399 lb", "400–499 lb", "500–599 lb",
            "600–699 lb", "700–799 lb", "800–899 lb"]
    bin_starts = {b: 280 - i*15 for i, b in enumerate(bins)}
    start = pd.Timestamp("2020-01-01")
    for week_idx in range(0, 280):
        d = start + pd.Timedelta(weeks=week_idx)
        for cl in ("Steers", "Heifers"):
            for wb in bins:
                base = bin_starts[wb]
                trend = (week_idx / 280) * 50  # general upward
                seasonal = 8 * (1 - abs(d.isocalendar().week - 30) / 25)
                noise = rng.normal(0, 6)
                p = base + trend + seasonal + noise - (12 if cl == "Heifers" else 0)
                rows.append({
                    "auction_date": d,
                    "weight_bin": wb,
                    "class": cl,
                    "head_count": int(rng.integers(40, 400)),
                    "price_low": round(p - 8, 2),
                    "price_avg": round(p, 2),
                    "price_high": round(p + 8, 2),
                })
    df = pd.DataFrame(rows)
    data_is_placeholder = True
    data_source_label = "synthetic stand-in (no live data available)"

if data_is_placeholder:
    print("SYNTHETIC STAND-IN DATA — for layout only. Not real prices.")
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| label: data-shape

# Same filter and bin-derivation as the other two chart pages: Steers and
# Heifers, Medium and Large grade 1, collapsed to 100-lb bins from
# weight_break_low (with avg_weight as a fallback for older rows).

import pandas as pd

if not data_is_placeholder:
    slim = df[
        (df["class"].isin(["Steers", "Heifers"]))
        & (df["frame"] == "Medium and Large")
        & (df["muscle_grade"] == "1")
    ].copy()
    wb = slim["weight_break_low"]
    aw = slim["avg_weight"]
    raw_weight = wb.fillna(aw)
    slim = slim[raw_weight.notna()].copy()
    slim["bin_lo"] = ((raw_weight[raw_weight.notna()] // 100) * 100).astype(int)
    slim = slim[(slim["bin_lo"] >= 300) & (slim["bin_lo"] <= 800)]
    slim["bin_hi"] = slim["bin_lo"] + 99
    slim["weight_bin"] = slim.apply(
        lambda r: f"{int(r['bin_lo'])}–{int(r['bin_hi'])} lb", axis=1
    )
    work = slim[
        ["auction_date", "weight_bin", "class",
         "price_low", "price_avg", "price_high"]
    ].copy()
else:
    work = df.copy()
    work["price_low"] = work["price_avg"]
    work["price_high"] = work["price_avg"]

# Window metadata for the caption
work["auction_date"] = pd.to_datetime(work["auction_date"])
date_min = work["auction_date"].min()
date_max = work["auction_date"].max()
n_weeks = work["auction_date"].dt.date.nunique()
n_obs = len(work)
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| label: deflate-to-dec-2025

# Same deflation logic as the other two chart pages — see
# site/price-weight.qmd's deflate chunk for the full explanatory
# comment block.

import pandas as pd

BASIS_PERIOD = pd.Timestamp("2025-12-01")
cpi_basis_label = "December 2025"

deflation_available = False
if CPI_PATH.exists():
    cpi = pd.read_parquet(CPI_PATH)[["period", "cpi_u"]].copy()
    cpi["period"] = pd.to_datetime(cpi["period"])
    basis_rows = cpi[cpi["period"] == BASIS_PERIOD]
    if not basis_rows.empty:
        cpi_basis_value = float(basis_rows["cpi_u"].iloc[0])
        deflation_available = True
    else:
        fallback = cpi.sort_values("period").iloc[-1]
        cpi_basis_value = float(fallback["cpi_u"])
        cpi_basis_label = pd.to_datetime(fallback["period"]).strftime("%B %Y")
        deflation_available = True

if deflation_available:
    work["period_month"] = work["auction_date"].dt.to_period("M").dt.to_timestamp()
    cpi_lookup = cpi.set_index("period")["cpi_u"]
    work["cpi_u_month"] = work["period_month"].map(cpi_lookup)
    work["deflator"] = cpi_basis_value / work["cpi_u_month"]
    import sys as _sys
    missing_cpi = int(work["deflator"].isna().sum())
    if missing_cpi:
        print(
            f"[deflate] {missing_cpi} observations lack a CPI month-match; "
            "leaving nominal.", file=_sys.stderr,
        )
    work["deflator"] = work["deflator"].fillna(1.0)
    work["price_avg_real"] = work["price_avg"] * work["deflator"]
else:
    work["price_avg_real"] = work["price_avg"]
```

::: {.callout-important}
**Data source**: `{python} data_source_label` ·
**Window**: `{python} date_min.strftime('%Y-%m-%d')` to `{python} date_max.strftime('%Y-%m-%d')` (`{python} n_weeks` distinct auction weeks) ·
**Observations**: `{python} f"{n_obs:,}"` lots aggregated to one point per (week, class, bin) ·
**Grade shown**: Medium and Large, muscle grade 1 ·
**Deflation basis**: `{python} cpi_basis_label` CPI-U NSA (`CUUR0000SA0`).
:::

## Weekly trajectory

Each line is the average price for one weight class at each auction week from October 2017 to the most recent published report. The top panel shows Steers; the bottom shows Heifers. Multiple lots within a single (week, class, weight bin) are simple-averaged so each line has one point per week. Hover any point to see the exact value and the count of lots behind it.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-alt: "Weekly-trends chart for the Clovis, NM feeder-cattle auction. Two stacked panels (Steers above, Heifers below) plot $/cwt against an auction-date timeline running from October 2017 to the most recent published report. Each line traces one weight bin (300-399 through 800-899 lb) at weekly resolution; gaps in the line indicate weeks where no observations were published. Two button rows above the chart toggle year window and currency mode."

import plotly.graph_objects as go
from plotly.subplots import make_subplots


def _per_week(work_df, price_col):
    """One point per (auction_date, class, weight_bin) — simple mean of
    price across multiple lots within that group."""
    agg = (
        work_df.groupby(["class", "weight_bin", "auction_date"], as_index=False)
               .agg(price=(price_col, "mean"), n=(price_col, "count"))
    )
    return agg


# ---------- year windows ----------
_ad = work["auction_date"]
_anchor = _ad.max()
_anchor_year = int(_anchor.year)
YEAR_WINDOWS = [
    ("all",    "All",            _ad.min(),                                    _anchor),
    ("5y",     "Last 5 years",   _anchor - pd.DateOffset(years=5),             _anchor),
    ("3y",     "Last 3 years",   _anchor - pd.DateOffset(years=3),             _anchor),
    ("latest", f"{_anchor_year}", pd.Timestamp(_anchor_year, 1, 1),            _anchor),
]

# Color palette — same as seasonality.qmd so a reader switching between
# the two pages sees consistent visual identity for each weight class.
COLOR_BY_BIN = {
    "300–399 lb": "#6a3d9a",
    "400–499 lb": "#000000",
    "500–599 lb": "#2ca02c",
    "600–699 lb": "#d17a00",
    "700–799 lb": "#1f77b4",
    "800–899 lb": "#8b0000",
}
BIN_ORDER = [
    "300–399 lb", "400–499 lb", "500–599 lb",
    "600–699 lb", "700–799 lb", "800–899 lb",
]

fig = make_subplots(
    rows=2, cols=1,
    shared_xaxes=True,
    vertical_spacing=0.10,
    subplot_titles=(
        "NM Clovis Steers — Weekly $/cwt",
        "NM Clovis Heifers — Weekly $/cwt",
    ),
)


def _add_class(agg_df, cls, row, visible, show_legend):
    """Add 6 bin-traces for one class. Always emits 6 traces (empties for
    bins with no data) so the visibility mask index stays predictable.

    Date and price values are explicitly converted to ISO strings and
    floats — pandas Timestamp objects don't serialize cleanly to Plotly's
    JSON representation in Quarto's static-render path, which silently
    yields empty traces.
    """
    for wb in BIN_ORDER:
        sub = (
            agg_df[(agg_df["class"] == cls) & (agg_df["weight_bin"] == wb)]
            .sort_values("auction_date")
        )
        if sub.empty:
            fig.add_trace(
                go.Scatter(
                    x=[], y=[], name=f"{wb}",
                    legendgroup=wb, showlegend=False, visible=visible,
                ),
                row=row, col=1,
            )
            continue
        x_iso = pd.to_datetime(sub["auction_date"]).dt.strftime("%Y-%m-%d").tolist()
        y_float = pd.to_numeric(sub["price"], errors="coerce").astype(float).tolist()
        n_int = pd.to_numeric(sub["n"], errors="coerce").astype("Int64").tolist()
        fig.add_trace(
            go.Scatter(
                x=x_iso,
                y=y_float,
                mode="lines+markers",
                name=wb,
                legendgroup=wb,
                showlegend=show_legend,
                visible=visible,
                line=dict(color=COLOR_BY_BIN[wb], width=1.5),
                marker=dict(color=COLOR_BY_BIN[wb], size=4),
                customdata=n_int,
                hovertemplate=(
                    f"<b>{wb} · {cls}</b><br>"
                    "Week of %{x}<br>"
                    "Avg price: $%{y:.2f}/cwt<br>"
                    "n = %{customdata:,}<extra></extra>"
                ),
            ),
            row=row, col=1,
        )


# Trace order:
#   For each year_window in YEAR_WINDOWS:
#       For each mode in (nominal, real):
#           Steers (6 bin traces, legend on), Heifers (6, legend off)
# 12 traces per (year, mode) group, 8 groups, 96 traces total.
N_TRACES_PER_GROUP = 12
N_GROUPS = len(YEAR_WINDOWS) * 2
N_TRACES = N_GROUPS * N_TRACES_PER_GROUP

for y_idx, (y_key, y_label, lo_d, hi_d) in enumerate(YEAR_WINDOWS):
    sub = work[(_ad >= lo_d) & (_ad <= hi_d)]
    series_n = _per_week(sub, "price_avg")
    series_r = _per_week(sub, "price_avg_real")
    is_default = (y_idx == 0)
    _add_class(series_n, "Steers",  row=1, visible=is_default, show_legend=True)
    _add_class(series_n, "Heifers", row=2, visible=is_default, show_legend=False)
    _add_class(series_r, "Steers",  row=1, visible=False,      show_legend=True)
    _add_class(series_r, "Heifers", row=2, visible=False,      show_legend=False)


def _visibility_for(year_idx: int, mode_idx: int) -> list[bool]:
    out = [False] * N_TRACES
    group = year_idx * 2 + mode_idx
    start = group * N_TRACES_PER_GROUP
    for i in range(start, start + N_TRACES_PER_GROUP):
        out[i] = True
    return out


# Per-year x-axis range so each window's chart focuses tightly on the
# selected dates rather than always showing the full Oct 2017 → present
# axis with empty space on the left for narrower windows. Range values
# are ISO date strings to match the trace data and avoid pd.Timestamp
# serialization quirks.
def _xrange_for(year_idx: int) -> list:
    _, _, lo_d, hi_d = YEAR_WINDOWS[year_idx]
    return [lo_d.strftime("%Y-%m-%d"), hi_d.strftime("%Y-%m-%d")]


# ---------- two button rows: Mode (2) + Year window (4) ----------
# Same idiom as basis.qmd / price-weight.qmd / seasonality.qmd. The JS
# layer (below) keeps both rows' highlights independent.
NOMINAL_Y_TITLES = {
    "yaxis.title.text": "Avg price, $/cwt (nominal)",
    "yaxis2.title.text": "Avg price, $/cwt (nominal)",
}
REAL_Y_TITLES = {
    "yaxis.title.text": f"Avg price, $/cwt (base = {cpi_basis_label})",
    "yaxis2.title.text": f"Avg price, $/cwt (base = {cpi_basis_label})",
}
MODE_Y_TITLES = {0: NOMINAL_Y_TITLES, 1: REAL_Y_TITLES}

# Row 0 — Year window buttons. Each = (year × Nominal). Default = All.
year_row_buttons = []
for y_idx, (y_key, y_label, lo_d, hi_d) in enumerate(YEAR_WINDOWS):
    year_row_buttons.append(dict(
        label=y_label,
        method="update",
        args=[
            {"visible": _visibility_for(y_idx, 0)},
            {
                **NOMINAL_Y_TITLES,
                "xaxis.range": _xrange_for(y_idx),
                "xaxis2.range": _xrange_for(y_idx),
                "updatemenus[1].active": -1,  # clear mode row (fallback)
            },
        ],
    ))

# Row 1 — Mode buttons. Each = (All × mode). Default = Nominal.
# Note: clicking a Mode button preserves the All-window x-range; the JS
# layer overrides this to use the user's currently-selected year window.
mode_row_buttons = []
for m_idx, mode in enumerate(("nominal", "real")):
    label = "Nominal $" if mode == "nominal" else f"Real ({cpi_basis_label} $)"
    mode_row_buttons.append(dict(
        label=label,
        method="update",
        args=[
            {"visible": _visibility_for(0, m_idx)},
            {
                **MODE_Y_TITLES[m_idx],
                "xaxis.range": _xrange_for(0),
                "xaxis2.range": _xrange_for(0),
                "updatemenus[0].active": -1,  # clear year row (fallback)
            },
        ],
    ))

fig.update_layout(
    height=940,
    margin=dict(t=240, b=60, l=80, r=30),
    hovermode="x unified",
    paper_bgcolor="white",
    plot_bgcolor="white",
    font=dict(family="Helvetica, Arial, sans-serif", size=13, color="#222"),
    legend=dict(orientation="h", yanchor="bottom", y=1.035, xanchor="center", x=0.5),
    # APPEND to the make_subplots-generated subplot titles ("NM Clovis Steers
    # — Weekly $/cwt", "NM Clovis Heifers — Weekly $/cwt"); a bare
    # `annotations=[...]` would REPLACE them and the panel headers would
    # silently disappear.
    annotations=list(fig.layout.annotations) + [
        dict(text="<b>Mode</b>",
             showarrow=False, xref="paper", yref="paper",
             x=0.0, y=1.32, xanchor="left", yanchor="top",
             font=dict(size=12, color="#222")),
        dict(text="<b>Year window</b>",
             showarrow=False, xref="paper", yref="paper",
             x=0.0, y=1.20, xanchor="left", yanchor="top",
             font=dict(size=12, color="#222")),
    ],
    updatemenus=[
        dict(  # year row (index 0) — All highlighted by default
            type="buttons",
            direction="right",
            active=0,
            x=0.18, y=1.20,
            xanchor="left", yanchor="top",
            pad=dict(r=6, t=4, b=4),
            showactive=True,
            buttons=year_row_buttons,
        ),
        dict(  # mode row (index 1) — Nominal highlighted by default
            type="buttons",
            direction="right",
            active=0,
            x=0.18, y=1.32,
            xanchor="left", yanchor="top",
            pad=dict(r=6, t=4, b=4),
            showactive=True,
            buttons=mode_row_buttons,
        ),
    ],
)

# X-axis: time. Default range = "All" window. Year buttons override via
# args. The bottom panel carries the axis title so the shared x-axis
# isn't double-labeled.
fig.update_xaxes(
    type="date",
    showgrid=True, gridcolor="#e5e5e5",
    linecolor="#888", mirror=False, ticks="outside",
)
fig.update_xaxes(title_text="Auction date", row=2, col=1)

fig.update_yaxes(
    showgrid=True, gridcolor="#e5e5e5",
    tickprefix="$", linecolor="#888", mirror=False, ticks="outside",
    title_text="Avg price, $/cwt (nominal)",
)
fig.update_layout(yaxis2=dict(matches="y"))

fig.show()


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Independent-toggle JS layer (mirrors basis.qmd / price-weight.qmd /
# seasonality.qmd). The state table also carries the per-year x-axis range
# because this page's date axis needs explicit ranges to focus the chart
# on the selected window (Plotly's auto-range over date axes wastes
# horizontal space when narrowing from All to Last 3 years).

import json
from IPython.display import HTML, display

_state_table = {}
for _y in range(len(YEAR_WINDOWS)):
    for _m in range(2):
        _state_table[f"{_y}-{_m}"] = {
            "v": _visibility_for(_y, _m),
            "xr": _xrange_for(_y),
        }
_js_state = json.dumps(_state_table)
_js_y_titles = json.dumps({
    "0": "Avg price, $/cwt (nominal)",
    "1": f"Avg price, $/cwt (base = {cpi_basis_label})",
})

display(HTML(f"""
<script>
(function() {{
    var STATE = {_js_state};
    var Y_TITLES = {_js_y_titles};
    var current = {{ year: 0, mode: 0 }};

    function findChart() {{
        var divs = document.querySelectorAll('.plotly-graph-div');
        for (var i = divs.length - 1; i >= 0; i--) {{
            var d = divs[i];
            if (d && d._fullLayout && d._fullLayout.updatemenus
                && d._fullLayout.updatemenus.length === 2
                && !d.__wtToggleWired) {{
                var menus = d._fullLayout.updatemenus;
                var btnCounts = [menus[0].buttons.length, menus[1].buttons.length].sort();
                if (btnCounts[0] === 2 && btnCounts[1] === 4) return d;
            }}
        }}
        return null;
    }}

    function applyState(gd) {{
        var key = current.year + '-' + current.mode;
        var s = STATE[key];
        if (!s) return;
        var yTitle = Y_TITLES[String(current.mode)];
        Plotly.update(gd,
            {{ visible: s.v }},
            {{
                'yaxis.title.text': yTitle,
                'yaxis2.title.text': yTitle,
                'xaxis.range': s.xr,
                'xaxis2.range': s.xr,
                'updatemenus[0].active': current.year,
                'updatemenus[1].active': current.mode,
            }}
        );
    }}

    function attach() {{
        var gd = findChart();
        if (!gd) {{ setTimeout(attach, 150); return; }}
        gd.__wtToggleWired = true;

        gd.on('plotly_buttonclicked', function(ev) {{
            var nBtns = (ev.menu && ev.menu.buttons) ? ev.menu.buttons.length : 0;
            var dim = (nBtns === 2) ? 'mode'
                    : (nBtns === 4) ? 'year'
                    : null;
            if (!dim) return;
            var btnIdx = ev.active;
            if (typeof btnIdx !== 'number' || btnIdx < 0) {{
                btnIdx = ev.menu.buttons.indexOf(ev.button);
            }}
            current[dim] = btnIdx;
            setTimeout(function() {{ applyState(gd); }}, 0);
        }});
    }}

    if (document.readyState === 'loading') {{
        document.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded', attach);
    }} else {{
        attach();
    }}
}})();
</script>
"""))
```

## How to read this

**The trajectory is the answer, not a single point.** Producers reading this chart should look for the slope of the lines over the past few months — is the curve rising, flat, or falling? — rather than trying to extract a forecast from any individual week. Auction prices move on cattle availability, feed-cost expectations, and futures-market sentiment; the chart reports what the market already cleared, not what it will clear next.

**The vertical spread between weight classes is the price slide.** At any given week, the gap between the 400–499 lb line and the 700–799 lb line is the price discount per cwt that heavier calves take. That gap widens and narrows over the cycle; a producer deciding whether to retain ownership and put more weight on calves can read off the historical trade-off at the right of the chart.

**Real-dollar lines flatten the inflation effect.** Switch to the real (December 2025 $) view to see how prices have moved net of CPI. The 2021–2022 nominal run-up looks less dramatic in real dollars; the 2024 climb looks more so.

## Why this view exists alongside the other two

The chart shelf decomposes the same dataset along three orthogonal axes:

| Page | Aggregates | Answers |
|---|---|---|
| [Price–weight explorer](price-weight.qmd) | across time, per (weight, class) | *what does each weight class fetch on average?* |
| [Weekly seasonality](seasonality.qmd) | across years, per (week-of-year, weight, class) | *when in the year do these prices peak?* |
| Weekly trends (this page) | nothing across time — raw weekly observations | *how have prices moved week-by-week?* |

A producer looking at all three answers a different question on each: structure, intra-year pattern, and trajectory. Together they triangulate "where am I" without making any one chart carry the whole story.

## Download

**Clovis feeder-cattle archive** (latest MARS vintage):
[Parquet](../data/processed/clovis_latest.parquet) · [CSV](../data/processed/clovis_latest.csv) · [XLSX](../data/processed/clovis_latest.xlsx)

**CPI deflator** (BLS CPI-U NSA `CUUR0000SA0`, the deflation basis):
[Parquet](../data/processed/cpi_latest.parquet) · [CSV](../data/processed/cpi_latest.csv) · [XLSX](../data/processed/cpi_latest.xlsx)

**Manifest** (SHA-256 of every snapshot the pipeline has committed):
[clovis_MANIFEST.json](../data/processed/clovis_MANIFEST.json)

Parquet is the canonical format and the smallest on disk. CSV opens in any text editor or spreadsheet app. XLSX opens directly in Excel/Numbers/LibreOffice. See the [data catalog](data.qmd) for the full inventory and per-file row counts.

## See also

- [Price–weight explorer](price-weight.qmd) — the structure view.
- [Weekly seasonality](seasonality.qmd) — the intra-year pattern view.
- [Methodology → Clovis pipeline](methodology/clovis.qmd) — every filter, bin, and edge case behind the snapshot this page reads from.
- [Methodology → CPI deflation](methodology/cpi.qmd) — how the December-2025-basis deflator is computed.

## Roadmap

- [ ] Per-week head-weighted mean (not simple-average) for multi-lot weeks. The current chart simple-averages multiple lots within the same (week, class, bin); a head-weighted mean is more accurate. Methodology page documents the choice.
- [ ] Mark the most recent observation per line with a labeled dot ("Latest week: $X.XX"). Bridge to a future *sell-now compare* widget.
- [ ] Hover-band showing the 5-year range for the same week-of-year, so a reader can see at a glance whether the current week is high or low for its calendar position.
